evidence_quotes.quote: Selling software as a one off purchase and then shutting it off or turning it into a subscription is a scam.
If store button does not say "subscribe" - it should be treated as a product.
Sales of the software, that do not clearly indicate it's a subscription, should not be turned off by the seller/publisher at a later date.
a license agreement should not be 50 pages of legalese.
Nobody should be expected to hire a lawyer to install an android app.
anything anti consumer in there shouldn't be considered legally binding.
There shouldn't be a redefinition of what you're purchasing within it.
If right of a consumer is revoked full refund should be issued.
(aka. can't get a service/product if you don't agree even with one clause in the thing)

paragraphs.text: Sales of the software, that do not clearly indicate it's a subscription, should not be turned off by the seller/publisher at a later date. Same goes for hardware that requires software support to function (camera doorbells, baby cameras, etc). Also there should not be a contract change after purchase, aka. if I bought a camera which offered the ability to view the footage from an app for free seller can't sudenly move that functionality behind a paywall. In case manufacturers do not wish to support the product sold they should release an option for customer to retain the functionality by some sort of means (publish server files and update firmware to allow connection to custom server, allow app direct connect to IP of a camera to review footage, etc) If unfeasable a compromise could be a minimum support window from the last purchase of at least 5 years (view it as contract at which purchase was made should be viable for 5 years since it's date). Producing hardware and then rendering it useless because servers went down is not only anti-consumer, it also produces tons of e-waste. Selling software as a one off purchase and then shutting it off or turning it into a subscription is a scam. Finally, a license agreement should not be 50 pages of legalese. Nobody should be expected to hire a lawyer to install an android app. Also, as it is non-negotiable and made from position of power by the company (aka. can't get a service/product if you don't agree even with one clause in the thing) anything anti consumer in there shouldn't be considered legally binding. There shouldn't be a redefinition of what you're purchasing within it. If store button does not say "subscribe" - it should be treated as a product. When I go to netflix I know I'm paying for a subscription and that it will end, it's very clear on the page. However when I buy something from steam or app store my access to it can be revoked at any time even though nowhere it is clearly stated (except for fine print where it just says seller retains the right to revoke access to my license at their discression with any or no reason). If right of a consumer is revoked full refund should be issued.
